primer.markdown:
# From a blocking promise to a token-by-token async iterator

Without streaming, `messages.create` is one round-trip: you `await` the whole reply and print it at the end. For a CLI this is the wrong shape. The user stares at a blinking cursor for three seconds, then the entire reply lands in one paste. Streaming fixes that by returning an async iterable of *events* — you consume them with `for await (const event of stream)` and render each token as it arrives.

The mechanics are a single boolean flip. typescript/EXAMPLES.md lines 137-161 show the whole thing: pass `stream: true` to `messages.create` and the SDK returns an `AsyncIterable<MessageStreamEvent>` instead of a single `Message`. You iterate the stream; you filter for events where `event.type === "content_block_delta"` and `event.delta.type === "text_delta"`; you `process.stdout.write(event.delta.text)` for each one. That is the complete streaming loop.

Three things move from implicit to explicit once you stream. First, **event types** — `message_start`, `content_block_start`, `content_block_delta`, `content_block_stop`, `message_delta`, `message_stop` — and you only care about `content_block_delta` for rendering text. Second, **token usage** only finalises in the `message_delta` event at the end of the stream, so cost tracking attaches to that event specifically. Third, **errors** can arrive mid-stream, so the `try/catch` goes around the `for await` loop, not around the initial `create` call. Everything else — the model ID, the messages, the `max_tokens` cap — is identical to the non-streaming version from Module 01.
primer.key_insight: Streaming is not a different API. It is the same `messages.create` with `stream: true`, returning an async iterable whose `content_block_delta` events carry the text you render.

visual.alt: A timeline diagram with two horizontal tracks. Top track (blocking) shows a single wide block labelled 'await messages.create' spanning 3 seconds, then a single large payload dropping into stdout at the end. Bottom track (streaming) shows 'stream: true' then a sequence of small event capsules emerging every ~50ms — message_start, content_block_start, content_block_delta (x many), content_block_stop, message_delta, message_stop — each delta capsule emitting a single token to stdout in real time.
visual.alt_es: Un diagrama de linea temporal con dos pistas horizontales. La pista superior (bloqueante) muestra un unico bloque ancho etiquetado 'await messages.create' que abarca 3 segundos, y despues un unico payload grande cayendo a stdout al final. La pista inferior (streaming) muestra 'stream: true' seguido por una secuencia de capsulas de evento emergiendo cada ~50ms — message_start, content_block_start, content_block_delta (x muchas), content_block_stop, message_delta, message_stop — cada capsula delta emite un token individual a stdout en tiempo real.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner reads the annotated streaming loop, identifies the one field (`stream: true`) that flips the call from blocking to streaming, and can point to the event type (`content_block_delta`) that carries renderable text.

questions.prompt: In typescript/EXAMPLES.md lines 137-163, which single field turns the `messages.create` call into a streaming call?
questions.explanation: EXAMPLES.md line 148: `stream: true`. That one boolean flips the return type from a `Message` to an `AsyncIterable<MessageStreamEvent>`. Everything else in the call — `model`, `max_tokens`, `messages` — is identical to the non-streaming path from Module 01.

questions.prompt: The streaming loop filters events with two conditions (EXAMPLES.md lines 152-155). What is the filter looking for?
questions.explanation: EXAMPLES.md lines 152-155 filter for `content_block_delta` events whose `delta.type` is `text_delta`. Those are the events that carry renderable text. `message_start`, `content_block_start`, `content_block_stop`, `message_delta`, and `message_stop` also arrive, but the CLI renderer ignores them.

questions.prompt: After the filter matches, what does the example do with the token on EXAMPLES.md line 156?
options: `process.stdout.write(event.delta.text)` — writes the token to stdout immediately, with no buffering.
`console.log(event.delta.text)` — prints the token plus a trailing newline, buffered.
`stream.push(event.delta.text)` — re-emits the token into a downstream channel.
questions.explanation: EXAMPLES.md line 156: `process.stdout.write(event.delta.text)`. `write` (unlike `console.log`) does not add a trailing newline, which is what you want for token-by-token rendering — each `text_delta` is a fragment, and fragments concatenate into a word. The trailing `console.log("\n")` on line 160 adds the single final newline after the stream completes.
